text: The lower esophageal sphincter or LES is a one-way valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach. When food or liquid is swallowed, the LES opens transiently to allow passage into the stomach. Otherwise, the sphincter remains closed to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus. However, for patients who suffer from reflux, the LES abnormally remains open allowing stomach secretions and contents to reflux up into the esophagus causing symptoms. A hiatal hernia may also be present further promoting abnormal reflux. A hiatal hernia is when the top of the stomach gets displaced or herniates through the diaphragm and up into the chest cavity. Given this anatomic shift, the overall esophageal length is now shorter. Additionally, the lower esophageal sphincter is no longer supported by the diaphragm to help with its sphincteric function. Normally, the LES is positioned at the same level as the diaphragm.

The surgeon repairs the hiatal hernia by repositioning the stomach below the diaphragm and reducing the diaphragmatic opening. A mesh is placed over the incision to reinforce the closure. The lower esophageal sphincter is tightened by wrapping the top part of the stomach around it in a 360-degree fashion, completing the Nissen fundoplication.

The surgical footage shows the dissection of the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissues. The surgeon carefully identifies the vagal nerves to avoid injury during the mobilization. The esophagus is mobilized to ensure at least three centimeters of the distal esophagus is positioned within the abdomen, achieved through high mediastinal dissection.

The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic hiatus with sutures, ensuring a tension-free repair. A mesh is placed over the incision to reinforce the closure and minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

The surgeon manipulates the freed esophagus and stomach, looping a rubber band around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) with the esophagus above and stomach below. A white mesh is visible at the top, indicating the site of hiatal hernia repair. The top part of the stomach is pushed behind and around the esophagus, moved back and forth to ensure looseness and prevent restricted blood flow. The stomach wrap is sutured with the first suture connecting stomach tissue to stomach tissue, the second suture passing through the esophagus, and the final suture through the LES, completing the Nissen fundoplication.
